text.title: Agency Tiered-Proposal System Core Rules
text.summary: Five hard rules for tiered-pricing proposals that convert inbound leads to retainers.

rule.statement: Every proposal has exactly 3 tiers: Good / Better / Best. Not 2 (no anchor), not 4 (decision paralysis). Tier names may vary (Essentials / Core / Premium) but the count is fixed.

rationale.source: Dan Ariely - decoy effect (Predictably Irrational, 2008); McKinsey B2B pricing research 2023

rule.rationale: Three-option pricing shifts the buyer from "yes/no" to "which one". McKinsey 2023 service-business data: 3-tier proposals close 35-45% vs. single-price 18-25%. Two-tier loses the anchor effect; four-tier triggers paralysis (analysis-paralysis effect documented by Iyengar).

rule.statement: The "Better" middle tier is designed to be the buyer's choice. Pricing: Good = 60-70% of Better, Best = 150-180% of Better. Scope: Better covers all stated needs; Good cuts a clearly-stated thing; Best adds something specific the buyer asked about.

rationale.source: Ariely decoy effect; Hubspot agency sales analysis 2024

rule.rationale: The middle tier is the strategy. Good exists so buyer doesn't pick lowest by default. Best exists so Better looks reasonable. If 80% of buyers pick Good, the Better tier is mis-anchored — raise its scope or lower its price. If 80% pick Best, Better is too thin.

rule.statement: Each tier lists exact deliverables, hours, SLA, and out-of-scope examples. No "ongoing support" without naming what that means in hours/responses/scope. Vagueness in proposal = renegotiation in month 3.

rationale.source: Mike Monteiro - Design Is a Job (2012); statement-of-work discipline

rule.rationale: Vague scope is the agency-founder's tax: every unclear sentence costs 2-5 hours of dispute later. Concrete scope means the client signs what they actually want. The proposal IS the SOW for the chosen tier.

rule.statement: Proposal sent within 48 hours of the discovery call. Past 48h, momentum decays — buyer talked to 2 other vendors and your specifics fade. Past 7 days, buyer has often chosen someone else.

rationale.source: HubSpot 2024 sales-velocity data; Win Without Pitching workflow

rule.rationale: HubSpot data: agency proposals sent within 48h close at 2.3x the rate of those sent in 4-7 days. The "I'm too busy" reasoning is the largest hidden cost in small-agency sales. Proposal speed is a strategic advantage.

rule.statement: Every retainer tier specifies a 6-month minimum commitment (or month-to-month with a 20-30% premium). Retainers shorter than 6 months underperform: ramp-up cost eats month 1, value compounds slowly. 6-month minimum aligns vendor + client incentives.

rationale.source: agency retainer ROI analyses; Profit First retainer math

rule.rationale: Without minimum, the buyer churns when the first sprint underdelivers (which it always does — ramp-up). 6 months gives the work time to compound. Month-to-month + premium offers buyer flexibility for an explicit cost.

example.title: 3-tier comparison for a content marketing retainer
example.code:
Good Better Best $3,500/mo $5,800/mo $9,500/mo
Articles/mo 2 long-form 4 long-form 6 long-form + 2 case studies SEO audit Quarterly Monthly Monthly + competitive intel Distribution Newsletter only Newsletter + X All channels + paid amplification Reporting Monthly Bi-weekly Weekly + Slack channel Strategy calls Quarterly Monthly Bi-weekly Minimum term 6 months 6 months 6 months Out of scope Video, podcast, design (available as add-ons)
The "Better" tier is designed to be the choice. "Good" cuts strategy calls (painful for engaged buyers). "Best" adds paid amplification (a stretch that signals capability ceiling).
example.note: Illustrates r1 (3 tiers), r2 (anchoring), r3 (fixed scope), r5 (6-month minimum).

example.title: Single-price proposal
example.code:
"Our content marketing retainer is $5,800/month. We'll do articles, SEO, and reporting."
Problem: violates r1 (no tiers), r3 (vague scope). Buyer asks "what if we want fewer articles?" Now you're negotiating downward from a single anchor. Repair: 3-tier system. The negotiation is "which tier" not "how cheap".
example.note: Single-price is the canonical pattern that loses agencies 20-30% of their inbound conversion.
